title: Rustic X Desk
summary: A 5-foot farmhouse desk on three X-detailed leg sets, with a bookshelf built into one end and a 2x6 plank top — all construction lumber, mostly pocket holes.
description:
This is a desk-sized version of the farmhouse X furniture family: three 2x4 leg sets, X detailing scribed into the openings, a 1x10 shelf turning one end into a bookcase, and a fat 2x6 plank top. All of it is construction lumber — studs, furring strips, and a single 1x10 — which is how a 59" desk with this much presence stays firmly in budget territory.

The X detailing is the part that scares people, and the source's method is the honest one: don't calculate the angles, SCRIBE them. Lay the 2x2 across the assembled leg opening, mark where it crosses the frame on both ends, and cut to your marks on the miter saw. Every opening is slightly different and scribing absorbs that automatically; trigonometry doesn't. The Xs are glue-and-brad trim, not structure, so a hairline gap at one tip is a character mark, not a failure.

The build order matters more than it looks: the middle shelf's cleats go on only two of the three leg sets, the shelf boards get pocket-screwed into a panel and hung between those two, and only then does the third leg set join the party via 2x2 stretchers cut to your actual measured gap ('ABOUT 18-1/2"' in the cut list means exactly that — measure yours). The three 2x2s across the chair area stiffen the knee span without boxing your legs in.

Build the top as its own panel — 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws edge-joining four 2x6s — then drop the base onto it upside down and attach. Pick the four straightest 2x6s in the store for it; a crowned plank in a desktop rocks every coffee cup you ever set down.

tools.note: Both settings: 1-1/2" for the 2x stock, 3/4" for the 1x10 shelf.
The scribed X pieces want a saw that swings to odd angles repeatably.
2" brads set the X detailing and cleats.
Construction lumber needs a real sanding schedule before it's furniture.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft or stud length
materials.note: Leg sets. Studs (92-5/8") cut the parts just fine.

materials.name: 2x6, 10 ft
materials.note: The desktop. Pick the four straightest, driest boards in the rack — each 10-footer yields two top planks.

materials.name: 2x2 furring strip, 8 ft
materials.note: X detailing, cleats, and stretchers. Furring strips are fine; cull the bananas.

materials.name: 1x10, 6 ft
materials.note: The end shelf — two boards pocket-screwed into a panel.

materials.name: Stain and topcoat
materials.note: A desk top earns a durable topcoat — wipe-on poly at minimum.

cutList.note: Rough length — scribe each piece in its opening and cut to the marks.
ABOUT 18-1/2" — measure the real gap between your leg sets and cut to that.

steps.title: Build three identical leg sets
steps.body:
Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes on each end of the 17-3/4" rails. Attach a rail flush at the top of each leg pair and one 3" up from the bottom, pocket holes facing what will be the outside (the X detailing hides some; the shelf and top hide the rest). 2-1/2" pocket screws throughout.

Build all three as clones and stack them to check — the desk sits flat only if the leg sets agree with each other.

steps.title: Scribe and fit the X detailing
steps.body:
Lay a 2x2 diagonally across a leg-set opening, mark where the frame crosses it at both ends, and cut to the marks on the miter saw. Fit the full diagonal first, then scribe the two half pieces that meet it.

Don't chase the angles with math — scribing self-corrects for each opening's real dimensions. Glue and 2" brads. Do all three leg sets and accept that the Xs are trim: a whisper of gap at a tip disappears under stain.

steps.title: Cleats for the shelf end
steps.body: Brad-nail and glue a 2x2 cleat across the inside of TWO leg sets only — these two become the bookshelf end of the desk. The third leg set stays cleatless for the knee space.

steps.title: Panel up and hang the shelf
steps.body:
Pocket-screw two 1x10 boards edge-to-edge (3/4" holes, 1-1/4" screws) into a shelf panel, then drill 3/4" pocket holes around all four sides — two per side.

Rest the panel on the cleats between the two shelf-end leg sets and screw it to the sides through those holes. The shelf is doing double duty: book storage and the rigid box that squares that whole end of the desk.

steps.title: Bridge to the third leg set
steps.body:
Measure the actual space between the shelf assembly and where the third leg set lands, cut the six 2x2 stretchers to that measurement, and drill a single 1-1/2" pocket hole in each end. Connect with 2-1/2" pocket screws and glue, and tie the shelf to its adjacent stretchers with the 1-1/4" screws through the holes you already drilled.

Then add the three long 2x2s across the chair area — they keep the knee span from racking without putting a rail where your legs go.

steps.title: Build the top and land the base
steps.body:
Edge-join the four 2x6 planks with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws, clamping each seam flat as you drive. Flip the finished top face-down on a blanket, center the base upside down on it, and screw the base to the underside.

Sand hard — knock down every plank seam, break every edge — then stain and topcoat. The top takes elbows, keyboards, and hot mugs; give it two coats more than you think it needs.

images.alt: Rustic farmhouse desk with X-detailed legs and built-in end shelf
